response:
Problems found
==============

1. Missing imports  
   the function uses os and time but never imports them; it only imports shutil inside the function. The first call to os.listdir will raise NameError.

2. Removes files with shutil.rmtree un-conditionally  
   shutil.rmtree() is meant for directories.  
   • If path is a regular file it raises NotADirectoryError.  
   • If path is a FIFO, socket, device, etc. it raises ValueError.

3. Symlink attack / directory traversal  
   rmtree follows symbolic links. An attacker who can create a symlink inside directory could point it to “/etc”, “c:\Windows”, a network share, etc. When your cleanup runs it will erase outside the intended tree.

4. Time-of-check / time-of-use (TOCTOU) race  
   You look up mtime and later delete the object. Between those two system calls the file can be swapped for a different one (again possible by an attacker with write access). The program would then erase something whose age you never checked.

5. No error handling  
   Any exception (permission denied, read-only FS, network glitch) aborts the whole cleanup leaving the rest of the work undone.

6. In-function import / performance  
   Importing every time the function is called is wasteful. Do it once at module load.

7. Inefficient repeated calculations  
   days * 86400 and time.time() are recomputed on every iteration.

8. No validation that directory is really a directory or even exists.

9. Lacks logging/audit trail so you cannot know what was removed.

10. Style issues  
    • PEP-8 discourages single-letter variable names in non-trivial code.  
    • Use of os.listdir forces an extra stat() call per entry; os.scandir / pathlib gives you the stat result for free.

Fixes / hardened version
------------------------

```python
import os
import time
import shutil
import logging
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Union

SECONDS_IN_DAY = 24 * 60 * 60   # readability

def cleanup_old_files(directory: Union[str, os.PathLike], days: int = 30) -> None:
    """
    Delete files *and* directories inside *directory* older than *days* days.
    Symlinks are skipped to avoid accidentally deleting outside the tree.
    """
    base_path = Path(directory).resolve()
    if not base_path.is_dir():
        raise NotADirectoryError(base_path)

    # compute deadline once
    deadline = time.time() - days * SECONDS_IN_DAY

    # os.scandir / Path.iterdir both return dir-entries with cached stat info
    for entry in base_path.iterdir():
        try:
            # -------- security checks ----------
            if entry.is_symlink():
                logging.warning("Skipping symlink %s", entry)
                continue

            # use stat obtained without following symlinks
            mtime = entry.stat(follow_symlinks=False).st_mtime
            if mtime > deadline:
                continue   # still fresh

            # -------- deletion ----------
            if entry.is_dir():
                shutil.rmtree(entry)
                logging.info("Removed directory %s", entry)
            else:
                entry.unlink()
                logging.info("Removed file %s", entry)

        # catch only expected problems so we do not mask programming errors
        except (PermissionError, FileNotFoundError, OSError) as exc:
            logging.error("Could not remove %s: %s", entry, exc)
```

What the changes address
------------------------

• Imports are at module level.  
• pathlib / iterdir gives clearer semantics.  
• Deadline is computed once.  
• unlink() for files; rmtree() only for real directories.  
• Symlinks are skipped (alternatively, unlink them instead of following).  
• stat() is taken with follow_symlinks=False, eliminating one attack vector.  
• All file operations are wrapped in try/except to keep processing even when one entry fails and to create an audit trail.  
• Function validates its input and logs important actions.  
• Code style meets PEP-8 and is easier to maintain.
